bibliographic_data.title: Assessing the Viability of Quantum-Resistant IKEv2 over Constrained and Internet-Scale Networks
bibliographic_data.abstract: Within 1–2 decades, quantum computers may become powerful enough to break current public-key cryptography, prompting authorities such as the IETF and NIST to push for adopting quantum-resistant cryptography (QRC) in ecosystems like Internet Protocol Security (IPsec). Yet, IPsec struggles to adopt QRC, primarily because Internet Key Exchange Protocol Version 2 (IKEv2), which sets up IPsec sessions, cannot easily tolerate the large public keys and digital signatures of QRC. Many IETF RFCs have been proposed to integrate QRC into IKEv2, but their performance and interplay remain largely untested in practice. In this paper, we measure the performance of these RFCs over constrained links by developing a flexible, reproducible measurement testbed for IPsec with quantum-resistant IKEv2 proposals. Deploying our testbed in lossy wireless links and on the internationally distributed FABRIC testbed for Internet scenarios, we reveal that bottlenecks arise with quantum-resistant IKEv2 under high round-trip times, non-trivial packet loss, or other constraints. Our results, including the revelation of a 400–1000-fold increase in data overhead over high-loss wireless links, expose the shortcomings of today’s RFCs and call for further work in this vital area of post-quantum network security.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper measures the performance of proposed IETF RFCs for integrating quantum-resistant cryptography into IKEv2 over constrained links, revealing significant data overhead and bottlenecks under high round-trip times and packet loss.
core_content.key_methodology: Developed a flexible, reproducible measurement testbed to evaluate the performance of quantum-resistant IKEv2 proposals in various network conditions, including lossy wireless links and internationally distributed FABRIC testbeds.
outcomes: Revealed that quantum-resistant IKEv2 can require over 1000 times more data transmission than classical cryptography on severely impaired wireless links.
Identified performance issues with existing RFC proposals in constrained network environments.
core_content.full_text_markdown: The paper addresses the challenge of integrating quantum-resistant cryptography (QRC) into Internet Key Exchange Protocol Version 2 (IKEv2), which is crucial for establishing secure IPsec sessions. The authors propose a measurement strategy to evaluate the performance of various IETF RFCs that aim to integrate QRC into IKEv2, particularly in constrained network environments such as lossy wireless links and internationally distributed testbeds like FABRIC. Through their experiments, they found significant data overhead and bottlenecks when using quantum-resistant cryptography under high round-trip times and packet loss conditions. This work highlights the need for further research to address these challenges and improve post-quantum network security.
